quote: Hey, man. This is Jake with Ion. I was just giving you a call here because I saw that you requested some information on solar for your house.
stewart_read: Jake's opening never deploys the Tesla-certified-installer framing the schema prescribes for Tesla-sourced leads. He introduces Ion as a generic solar company and references a form submission, not the Tesla referral. This is the original sin of the call — the legitimacy anchor that would have answered 'I'm not familiar with Ion' before Larry had to ask it. The Tesla framing (per intros.tesla) exists precisely to answer 'is this a scam?' in the first 30 seconds. Jake skipped it.
alternative_hypothesis: Strategic read: Jake may have avoided Tesla framing intentionally to prevent the product-expectation conversation early. Not supported — the product-expectation conversation happened anyway, and without the legitimacy anchor it was harder to resolve.
coaching_implication: Open the replay at 00:09. Ask Jake: 'What's the first thing we want Larry to know about us?' Then walk him through the Tesla-certified-installer intro as the answer to 'I'm not familiar with Ion' before the customer has to ask. This is a mechanics fix, not a philosophy fix.

quote: I think I have to have more trees taken down Oh, yeah. Before beforehand.
stewart_read: Larry volunteers — unprompted — that he has already had trees removed and has eight more scheduled. This is a customer actively managing his property to make solar viable. It is the clearest enthusiasm signal on the call. Jake acknowledges it ('Okay') and pivots to address confirmation rather than anchoring on it. The correct move here is to reflect the signal back: Larry has been planning this for a while and is removing obstacles himself — that is a buyer orientation, not a browser orientation.
coaching_implication: Show Jake this moment and ask him what it tells us about Larry as a customer. Use it to illustrate what 'qualifying on interest depth' looks like per verify.interest_in_solar — the Schema says top setters spend 60+ seconds here. Larry gave Jake an open door; Jake walked past it.

quote: We we typically get the numbers from the lead sources. We don't always control what they, put out, unfortunately.
stewart_read: Larry has just named 'false advertisement on Facebook' as the source of his information and implicitly accused Ion of being connected to it. Jake's response distances Ion from responsibility but does not repair the trust gap. The schema protocol for 'how did you get my number' calls for honest specificity: name the referral source cleanly and anchor Ion's legitimacy separately. Jake did neither — he issued a partial disclaimer and pivoted to the product. The result is that Larry's suspicion remains unaddressed and resurfaces at 07:21.
alternative_hypothesis: Strategic read: Jake may have moved quickly past the data-origin question to avoid dwelling on a topic he cannot fully resolve. Not supported — the topic recurs twice more, which is the consequence of not resolving it here.
coaching_implication: This is the trust repair moment Jake needed. Suggested reframe (intent, not verbatim): 'You filled out a form through Tesla's referral network — Tesla routes inquiries to us as their installer partner in Virginia. That's how we got connected. I know that's different from what the ad may have said, and I want to be straight with you about it.' Two sentences of honesty here likely prevent the data-mining exchange at 07:38.

quote: Is that something y'all do? It's something I think I could probably do myself, actually.
stewart_read: Larry's ground-mount question is his peak curiosity moment — he has observed a neighbor's installation, identified a technical workaround for his east-west roof, and is actively problem-solving. This is an enthusiasm signal and a scope-creep trap simultaneously. The enthusiasm: Larry is genuinely interested in solar if the technical fit exists. The trap: Jake answered the question directly ('we actually don't do the ground mount systems anymore') instead of bridging it to the specialist who can actually address it. This triggered the roof-leak warranty debate that followed — a 90-second exchange Jake did not win.
alternative_hypothesis: Strategic read: Jake may have wanted to close off ground mounts quickly to keep the conversation on roof-mount systems where Ion can deliver. Partially plausible, but the execution (flat denial + unsolicited warranty defense) closed off Larry's curiosity rather than redirecting it.
coaching_implication: Use this moment to teach the specialist bridge. Suggested reframe (intent, not verbatim): 'Ground mounts are worth exploring — that's a design question our specialist can work through with your actual site data. Want me to get you on with them?' The bridge converts the technical question into a booking reason rather than a debate topic.

quote: Yeah. I mean, you got our number, so you can give us a callback if, it is something that you're kinda trying to look into when, you do get that roof. Alright?
stewart_read: This is the highest-revenue moment Jake missed. Larry has just said he cannot consider solar until he gets a new roof — and that the roof is scheduled but delayed by snow. The schema covers this exactly: a customer who needs a new roof is a roofing lead AND a deferred solar lead, not a lost contact. Ion does roofing in-house. Jake's response — 'you got our number, give us a callback' — is a passive surrender that produces zero revenue and leaves Larry without any reason to call back. The roofing bridge (per cross_sell_signals.roof_replacement_planned) would have converted a 'not ready for solar' into an immediate roofing conversation and a locked solar follow-up.
alternative_hypothesis: Strategic read: Jake assessed Larry as too skeptical to convert on this call and decided a soft exit was better than a hard push. Not supported — there is no evidence Jake knew about Ion's roofing offering. The more likely read is that Jake simply did not know the bridge existed.
coaching_implication: This is the primary teaching moment for the manager. Open the schema to cross_sell_signals.roof_replacement_planned and show Jake the exact bridge language. Then ask: 'What would a successful outcome look like here if we can't book a solar appointment today?' The answer is a roofing referral and a locked solar callback — not a 'give us a call.' This miss cost Ion a roofing sale and a solar pipeline entry.

quote: Yeah. It is it is a
stewart_read: Larry has just said data mining is 'the biggest money making thing in the world today' in a tone of resigned accusation. Jake's response — 'Yeah. It is' — is a full concession that aligns Jake with the adversarial frame Larry is using. This is the lowest trust point of the call. The schema protocol for this concern calls for honest specificity that reduces suspicion — not agreement with the customer's most hostile interpretation. Jake had no reframe prepared and defaulted to social agreement, which confirmed Larry's view of Ion as a data-mining operation.
alternative_hypothesis: Strategic read: Jake was trying to validate Larry's frustration to build rapport. Not supported — validating 'data mining is the biggest money-making thing in the world' does not build rapport with a customer who has named Ion as a participant in that system.
coaching_implication: Show Jake the contrast between protocols.how_did_you_get_my_number (honest specificity reduces suspicion) and what he actually said. Then ask: 'When a customer says something like this, what's the one thing we never want to do?' Agreement is the wrong move. A better orientation: acknowledge the frustration, separate Ion from the ad that misled him, and bring the conversation back to what Larry actually wanted — which was solar.

quote: Alright. Well, maybe I'll contact you after I, get a roof. Cool. You don't even have an idea of what what these are running?
stewart_read: Larry's 'maybe I'll contact you after I get a roof' is a soft callback — not a locked time, not a locked commitment. The schema's soft_outcome_distinction notes the major delta between a callback with a locked time and 'we'll touch base.' Jake accepts it without any attempt to convert it to a real follow-up structure. The follow-on pricing question is then answered with a partial explanation of the lease model — more scope creep — rather than being bridged to the specialist as the reason to book. This moment was the last window to convert a passive exit into an actionable next step.
alternative_hypothesis: Strategic read: Jake recognized the call was over and was managing a graceful exit. Plausible, but the strategic version of that exit would have locked the roofing referral and a specific callback date before hanging up.
coaching_implication: Ask Jake: 'What's the difference between a customer saying they'll call us back and us having a real next step?' Use this to introduce the locked-callback structure — even if the appointment is weeks out (pending the roof), the rep should set a specific date and send a confirmation text. Without a locked time, this lead is cold the moment the call ends.
